g.data-narration: Welcome to Finite Automata with Epsilon Transitions. This powerful extension allows spontaneous moves without consuming input.
An ε-NFA extends standard NFA by allowing transitions without consuming input symbols, called epsilon transitions.
g.text: A Finite Automaton with Epsilon Transitions extends NFA by allowing transitions that
occur without consuming any input symbol (ε-transitions).

g.data-narration: Epsilon transitions simplify automaton construction, especially when converting from regular expressions to finite automata.
g.text: • Handle optional patterns without explicit input

g.data-narration: An ε-NFA is formally defined as a 5-tuple M equals Q, Sigma, delta, q naught, F where delta maps to power set of Q.
The key difference: standard NFA uses Q times Sigma, while ε-NFA uses Q times Sigma union epsilon.
Epsilon closure of state q contains all states reachable by zero or more epsilon transitions, including q itself.
g.text: ε-closure(q) = { r | r is reachable from q via 0+ ε-transitions }

g.data-narration: To compute epsilon closure: start with the state, then add all states reachable through epsilon transitions recursively.
g.text: 1. Add q to the closure set
2. For each state p in closure with ε-transition p→r, add r and repeat

g.data-narration: Here is a visual example. From q0, we can reach q1 via epsilon, then q2 via epsilon. The closure is q0, q1, q2.
The extended transition function computes all states reachable after processing an entire input string using epsilon closures.
g.text: Computes set of states reachable after processing entire string w

g.data-narration: A string w is accepted if after processing it, the set of reachable states contains at least one final state.
g.text: String w is ACCEPTED if δ̂(q₀, w) ∩ F ≠ ∅
At least one final state must be reachable after processing the entire string

g.data-narration: Consider an ε-NFA accepting strings ending with 01. It uses epsilon to optionally skip to the processing state.
g.text: Example 1: ε-NFA for strings ending in "01"

g.data-narration: For string 101: start at q0, epsilon to q1, read 1 to q2, epsilon back to q1, read 0 to q1, read 1 to q2. Accepted!
g.text: Final state q₂ reached → String ACCEPTED ✓

g.data-narration: To convert ε-NFA to DFA: DFA states are subsets of ε-NFA states. Start with epsilon closure of initial state.
g.text: Converting ε-NFA to DFA: Subset Construction
Maximum DFA states: 2^n where n = number of ε-NFA states

g.data-narration: Let's convert: initial DFA state is epsilon closure of q0, which is q0, q1. Both transitions lead back to this set.
Importantly, ε-NFA, NFA, and DFA are all equivalent in computational power - they recognize exactly the same language class.
g.text: All three recognize exactly the same class of languages (Regular Languages)

g.data-narration: In summary: ε-NFA uses epsilon transitions for convenience, epsilon closure is fundamental, and conversion to DFA uses subset construction.
g.text: • ε-closure always contains the state itself
• Don't forget state itself in ε-closure
• Max 2^n DFA states from n ε-NFA states
